result.markdown:
--- title: "Questionnaire" bibliography: references.bib format: html: default docx: default echo: false always_allow_html: true --- ::: {.cell} ::: ::: callout-caution This is a draft for the questionnaire. Right now, the questionnaire is way too long. Please make suggestions to remove/add variables, or to adapt current ones. ::: # Consent form ::: callout-note Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: You are invited to participate in a research study that will ask about your opinions and attitudes. You must be at least 18 years of age to participate. There are no risks associated with this study and your identity will be kept confidential. We cannot and do not guarantee or promise that you will receive any benefits from this study. Participation If you decide to participate in this project, please understand your participation is voluntary and you may withdraw your consent or discontinue participation at any time without penalty. The alternative is not to participate. You have the right to refuse to answer particular questions. Your individual privacy will be maintained in all published and written data resulting from the study. Contact Information If you have any questions, concerns or complaints about this research, its procedures, risks and benefits, contact us at trustclimsci.megastudy\@gmail.com. If you agree to participate in this research, please click to the next screen and complete the questionnaire. # Attention checks/LLM detection ::: callout-warning This section does currently not contain LLM detection checks. Recent studies suggest that detecting LLM responses can be quite difficult [@westwoodPotentialExistentialThreat2025]. We count on your expertise to help us find effective checks. If you could point us to relevant references or inform us about best practices at your lab, we would greatly appreciate it. ::: ## Intro ::: callout-note Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: You will need to qualify for this study. You will find out if you qualify shortly. This study takes about 15 mins to complete. It has several sections requiring attention. Participating until the very end of this study and answering all questions is the only way we can use your responses. It is therefore very important to us that you answer the following questions honestly. Do you agree to pay attention and participate in all sections of this study? \[Yes, No\] ## Basic check ::: callout-note Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: To help us keep track of who is paying attention, please select "Somewhat disagree" in the options below. \[Strongly agree; Agree; Somewhat agree; Neither agree nor disagree; Somewhat disagree; Disagree; Strongly disagree\] ## Reading comprehension ::: callout-note Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: Please read the following short article. Officials in a midsize town have been working for four years on a plan to produce an event license to cover all of the major events that occur at the town’s local stadium, which hosts concerts and home sports games. The application would be submitted each January and list all events expected to occur at the stadium over the next 12 months. If an unlisted event emerges during the year, lawmakers could hold a special hearing on the event, or accept it without a hearing and add it into the existing license. To assist with this plan, lawmakers filed legislation that would change state licensing laws so that annual event licenses will expire within one year. “This makes a minor change to current law, which provides that all licenses issued shall expire on December 31 of each year,” a lawmaker said. What was the topic of the short article you just read about? \[Medical funding; Event licensing; Political polarization; City budgeting; Election monitoring policy; Campaign finance reform\] ### Other ## Transition Congratulations, you have qualified for the full study! As you proceed to the next section, please make sure you do not close out of this tab. You must complete the whole study to collect your payment. I understand I must complete the full study to collect my full earnings. # Pre-treatment ## Demographics ### Gender What is your gender? \[Male; Female; Other \[text box\]\] ### Age How old are you? ### Education #### Option A ::: callout-note From @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: What is the highest degree or level of school you have completed? \[Less than high school graduate; High school graduate, diploma or the equivalent (for example: GED); Some college credit, no degree; Trade/technical/vocational training; Associate degree; Bachelor's degree; Master's degree; Professional degree; Doctorate degree\] #### Option B ::: callout-note From @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025 ::: What is your highest completed level of education? \[Did not attend school; Primary education; Secondary education (e.g., high school); Higher education (e.g., university degree or higher education diploma)\] ### Religion How religious do you consider yourself to be? #### Affiliation What is your religious affiliation, if any? \[No religion; Christian (e.g., Catholic, Protestant, Orthodox); Muslim; Jewish; Hindu; Buddhist; Other religion (please specify)\] #### Religiosity \[Slider, 0 = "Not at all religious" to 1 0 "Extremely religious"\] ### Income #### Option A ::: callout-note From @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: Which of the following ranges includes your total annual household income from all sources? \[Less than \$25,000; \$25,000 to \$34,999; \$35,000 to \$49,999; \$50,000 to \$74,999; \$75,000 to \$99,999; \$100,000 to \$124,999; \$125,000 to \$149,999; \$150,000 to \$174,999; \$175,000 to \$199,999; \$200,000 to \$249,999; \$250,000 or more\] #### Option B Roughly, what is your total annual household income from all sources? \[Text box\] ### Urban rural ### Race ::: callout-note From @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: Please select which race / ethnicity you identify as. (Please select all that apply.) \[White / Caucasian; Black / African American; Hispanic / Latino; Asian / Asian American; Other \[text box\]\] ### Geographical location (postcode) ::: callout-note This could help us measure physical distance to science. We could, for example, assess the density of scientific institutions in a given postcode area. Or maybe, an even better measure would be measuring an average distance of a postcode to **all** major scientific institutions in the US. Will probably highly correlate with urban vs. rural measures, but could allow for interesting comparisons within urban areas. ::: What is your zip code? \[text box\] ## Moderator varaibles ### Conservatism ::: callout-note From @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: In general, do you think of yourself as ... \[Extremely liberal; Liberal; Slightly liberal; Moderate, middle of the road; Slightly conservative; Conservative; Extremely conservative\] ### Partisan identity ::: callout-note Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: #### Party Generally speaking, do you usually think of yourself as a Republican, a Democrat, an Independent, or other? \[Republican; Democrat; Independent; Other \[text box\]\] #### Intesity How important is being a \[Republican/Democrat/Independent/Other\] to you? Below is a range from 0 to 100 indicating how important this is to you. Click on any space within this range and a bar will appear. Feel free to move that bar around to the number that best represents your answer. \[Slider, 0 = "Not important at all" to 1 0 "Extremely important"\] ### Belief in climate change ::: callout-caution Note that we ask this both pre- and post-treatment. ::: #### Option A ::: callout-note From @vlasceanuAddressingClimateChange2024 ::: How accurate do you think this statement is? "Human activities are causing climate change." \[Slider, 0 = not at all accurate, 100 = extremely accurate\] #### Option B ::: callout-note From @voelkelRegisteredReportMegastudy2026 ::: See @tbl-temperature-beliefs above. ### Social dominance orientation ::: callout-note Adapted from the scale used in @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, which is based on @prattoSocialDominanceContext2013. Instead of the original 4-point scale, we use sliders from 0 to 100. ::: ::: {#tbl-sdo .cell tbl-cap='Social dominance orientation (SDO). Participants indicate how much they favor or oppose ideas about different social groups. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
There are many kinds of groups in the world (e.g., men and women, ethnic and religious groups, nationalities, political factions). How much do you favor or oppose the ideas about groups in general?
In setting priorities, we must consider all groups 0 = Extremely oppose … 100 = Extremely favor
We should not push for group equality 0 = Extremely oppose … 100 = Extremely favor
Group equality should be our ideal 0 = Extremely oppose … 100 = Extremely favor
Superior groups should dominate inferior groups 0 = Extremely oppose … 100 = Extremely favor
````` ::: ::: ### Subjective inequality ::: callout-note Adapted from @schmalorConstructSubjectiveEconomic2022. Instead of the original 7-point scale, we use sliders from 0 to 100. ::: ::: {#tbl-subjective-inequality .cell tbl-cap='Subjective perceptions of economic inequality. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
Please indicate how much you agree or disagree with the following statements about economic inequality.
Almost all the money that is earned goes to only a few people. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Besides those at the very top, no one else has much money at all. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Real opportunities to succeed in life are only available to the wealthy. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Only those at the top own any wealth at all. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
````` ::: ::: ### Trust in institutions (US context) ::: callout-caution Inspired by the institutional trust measure in the US General Social Survey (GSS). Instead of the original 3-point scale, we use sliders. We also use different wording. The original GSS wording is: "I am going to name some institutions in this country. As far as the people running these institutions are concerned, would you say you have a great deal of confidence, only some confidence, or hardly any confidence at all in them?" ::: ::: {#tbl-institutional-trust-gss .cell tbl-cap='Trust in major social and political institutions. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
For the following institutions, how much do you trust the people running those institutions?
Banks and financial institutions 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Major companies 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Organized religion 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Education 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Executive branch of the federal government 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Organized labor 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Press 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Medicine 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Television 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
U.S. Supreme Court 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Scientific community 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Congress 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Military 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
````` ::: ::: ### Psychological distance to science ::: callout-note Adapted from @veckalovPsychologicalDistanceScience. Instead of the original 7-point scale, we use sliders. ::: ::: {#tbl-psychological-distance-science .cell tbl-cap='Psychological distance to science. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. Items marked with (R) are reverse-coded. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
Please indicate how much you agree or disagree with the following statements about science and scientists.
Temporal distance
Science is mainly focused on the distant future. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Most of today’s science is concerned with solving problems of the distant future. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Scientists spend most of their time working on issues of the distant future. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
We will see the impact of science more in the distant future than we do in the present. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Social distance
The prospect of working as a scientist seems beyond my reach. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
I rarely interact with scientists in real life. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Scientists are very different from me. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
It would be difficult for me to meet with a scientist. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Hypothetical distance
Scientific knowledge is a reliable way to solve important issues. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Science provides accurate information about the world we live in. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
We can rely on science to deliver results that can be implemented in real life. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
I can see the effects of science, whether positive or negative, on the world. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Spatial distance
Science and scientific research play a big role in my local area. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Scientific research really contributes to my local area. (R) 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
People from my local area don’t become scientists. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Very few scientists live or work in my town. 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
````` ::: ::: ### Nature relatedness ::: callout-note Adapted from @nisbetNR6NewBrief2013 ::: 1. My ideal vacation spot would be a remote, wilderness area. 2. I always think about how my actions affect the environment. 3. My connection to nature and the environment is a part of my spirituality. 4. I take notice of wildlife wherever I am. 5. My relationship to nature is an important part of who I am. 6. I feel very connected to all living things and the earth. \[1 - Disagree strongly; 2 - Disagree a little; 3 - Neither agree Agree or disagree; 4 - Agree a little; 5 - Agree strongly\] # Interventions ## Transition You are now moving on to a different section of the study. Please answer the following questions to the best of your ability. Thank you. ::: callout-caution tbd ::: # Post-treatment ## Transition You are now moving on to the final section of the study. Please answer the following questions to the best of your ability. Thank you. ## Main coutcome ### Trust in climate scientists ::: callout-note Inspired by the measure used in @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025 for scientists in general ::: ::: {#tbl-trust .cell tbl-cap='Multi-dimensional measure of trust in climate scientists. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
We are interested in your general opinion about climate scientists. In answering the following questions, think about climate scientists specifically in the context of their work/research.
Competence
How incompetent or competent are most climate scientists? 0 = Very incompetent … 100 = Very competent
How unintelligent or intelligent are most climate scientists? 0 = Very unintelligent … 100 = Very intelligent
How unqualified or qualified are most climate scientists? 0 = Very unqualified … 100 = Very qualified
Integrity
How dishonest or honest are most climate scientists? 0 = Very dishonest … 100 = Very honest
How unethical or ethical are most climate scientists? 0 = Very unethical … 100 = Very ethical
How insincere or sincere are most climate scientists? 0 = Very insincere … 100 = Very sincere
Benevolence
How unconcerned or concerned are most climate scientists about people’s wellbeing? 0 = Very unconcerned … 100 = Very concerned
How uneager or eager are most climate scientists to improve others’ lives? 0 = Very uneager … 100 = Very eager
How inconsiderate or considerate are most climate scientists of others’ interests? 0 = Very inconsiderate … 100 = Very considerate
Openness
How open, if at all, are most climate scientists to feedback? 0 = Not open … 100 = Very open
How unwilling or willing are most climate scientists to be transparent? 0 = Very unwilling … 100 = Very willing
How little or much attention do climate scientists pay to others' views? 0 = Very little attention … 100 = A great deal of attention
````` ::: ::: ## Secondary outcomes ### Donation behavior ::: callout-note To assess consequential and impactful behavior, participants will be given the option to donate a monetary bonus to the American Geophysical Union (AGU), or keep it for themselves. The AGU was chosen because it is the leading scientific association for scientists working on climate change. ::: For the next question, we would like you to have the opportunity to donate money to a non-profit or to keep it for yourself. Please decide how you would like to allocate \$10 between yourself and the American Geophysical Union (AGU). The AGU is a non-profit, non-partisan scientific association. It is the largest scientific association in the area of climate change in the US, with over 15,000 scientist members. You can give all of the money to the AGU and keep none for yourself, or you can keep all the money for yourself and give none to AGU, or you can pick any split in between. Note: We will randomly select a subset of 100 participants and realize their choices. \[Slider, 0\$ to 10\$\] ### Non-Political Behavioral Intentions ::: callout-note From @voelkelRegisteredReportMegastudy2026 ::: ::: {#tbl-nonpolitical-behavior .cell tbl-cap='Non-political behavioral intentions related to climate change. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
How likely are you to engage in the following activities in the next twelve months?
Eat less meat 0 = Not likely at all … 100 = Extremely likely
Walk, bicycle, carpool, or take public transportation more often instead of driving a vehicle by yourself 0 = Not likely at all … 100 = Extremely likely
Use reusable bags more often when you visit the grocery store 0 = Not likely at all … 100 = Extremely likely
Go on less personal (non-business) air travel 0 = Not likely at all … 100 = Extremely likely
Eat more local food (produced within 100 miles) 0 = Not likely at all … 100 = Extremely likely
Use less plastic 0 = Not likely at all … 100 = Extremely likely
````` ::: ::: ### Scientists' role in policy making ::: callout-note Scales were adapted from the "Normative perceptions of science and society" scale in @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, but slightly modified: We removed two items, one because it was not strictly true ("Climate scientists should remain independent from the policy-making process") and the other because it was not policy related ("Climate scientists should communicate their findings to the general public"). We also use sliders instead of a 5-point likert scale. ::: ::: {#tbl-policy-role .cell tbl-cap='Scientists\' role in policy making. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
To what extent do you agree or disagree with the following statements?
Climate scientists should work closely with politicians to integrate scientific results into policy-making 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Climate scientists should actively advocate for specific policies 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Climate scientists should communicate their findings to politicians 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Climate scientists should be more involved in the policy-making process 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
````` ::: ::: ### Reliance on scientists for lifestyle choices ::: callout-note Adapted from the "Willingness to be vulnerable to scientists" scale in @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, but heavily modified. We retain only one item, as the other two ("How much or little should governments rely on climate scientists’ guidance when making decisions related to climate science?" and "How much or little control do you want climate scientists to have on government decisions related to science?") were conceptually closer to scientists' role in policy making. We also make changes to the one item we retain (original: "How willing are you to rely on climate scientists’ advice when making lifestyle choices (e.g., sustainable transportation, energy use and nutrition)? (1) Not at all, (2) A little, (3) A moderate amount, (4) A lot, (5) A great deal"), as the endpoints of the original likert scale might be asymmetric. Our modification also aligns better with our scale for measuring scientists' role in policy making. ::: To what extent do you agree or disagree with the following statement? "When making lifestyle choices (e.g., sustainable transportation, energy use and nutrition), I am willing to rely on climate scientists’ advice." \[Slider, 0 = not at all … 100 = Strongly agree\] ### Single item trust in climate scientists ::: callout-note We add a single item question on trust in climate scientists, to compare how this maps onto the different dimensions of our multi-dimension main outcome measure. ::: "How much do you trust climate scientists?" \[Slider, 0 = not at all … 100 = very strongly\] ### Single item distrust in climate scientists ::: callout-note We add an item on distrust and comparing it to the trust item will allow us to test if they are symmetrical or not. ::: "How much do you **distrust** climate scientists?" \[Slider, 0 = not at all … 100 = very strongly\] ### Support for general climate policies ::: callout-note From @voelkelRegisteredReportMegastudy2026 ::: ::: {#tbl-general-climate-policy .cell tbl-cap='Support for general climate change mitigation policies. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
How much do you oppose or support the following statements.
The U.S. government should do more to reduce greenhouse gas emissions 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
The U.S. government should do more to increase energy efficiency 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
The U.S. government should do more to reduce global warming 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
````` ::: ::: ### Support for specific climate policies #### Option A ::: callout-note Adapted from @vlasceanuAddressingClimateChange2024. We use sliders instead of the original 3-point scale. Note that a reduced version of this has been used in @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025 (the first 5 items in @tbl-policy-support-vlasceanu) ::: ::: {#tbl-policy-support-vlasceanu .cell tbl-cap='Support for climate policies. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
How much do you support the following policies.
Raising carbon taxes on fossil fuels (e.g., gas, oil, coal) 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Expanding infrastructure for public transportation 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Increasing the use of sustainable energy such as wind and solar energy 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Protecting forested and land areas 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Increasing taxes on carbon-intensive foods (e.g., beef and dairy products) 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Increasing the number of charging stations for electric vehicles 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Increasing taxes on airline companies to offset carbon emissions 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Investing more in green jobs and businesses 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Introducing laws to keep waterways and oceans clean 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
````` ::: ::: #### Option B ::: callout-note From @voelkelRegisteredReportMegastudy2026 ::: ::: {#tbl-specific-climate-policy .cell tbl-cap='Support for specific climate change mitigation policies. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
How much do you oppose or support the following policies.
Require electric utilities to produce more electricity from renewable energy sources (this policy would decrease greenhouse gas emissions but might increase energy costs for the average household) 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
Ban the construction of new coal-fired power plants (this policy would decrease greenhouse gas emissions but might increase energy costs for the average household) 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
Release new federal oil and gas permits to increase the production of fossil fuels (this policy would increase greenhouse gas emissions but might decrease energy costs for the average household) 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
Remove existing direct U.S. federal and state subsidies to oil and gas companies (this policy would decrease greenhouse gas emissions but might increase energy costs for the average household) 0 = Strongly oppose … 100 = Strongly support
````` ::: ::: ### Funding for research on climate change ::: callout-note Adapted from the National [Science Foundation (NSF) – Science & Engineering Indicators](https://ncses.nsf.gov/pubs/nsb20227/public-perceptions-of-science-and-technology#general-perceptions-of-s-t), also used in @ophirFactorsAssessingSciences2023. ::: "Climate change research that advances the frontiers of knowledge is necessary and should be supported by funding from the federal government." \[Slider, 0 = Strongly disagree, 100 = Strongly agree\] ### Belief in climate change ::: callout-caution Note that we ask this both pre- and post-treatment. ::: #### Option A ::: callout-note From @vlasceanuAddressingClimateChange2024 ::: How accurate do you think this statement is? "Human activities are causing climate change." \[Slider, 0 = not at all accurate, 100 = extremely accurate\] #### Option B ::: callout-note From @voelkelRegisteredReportMegastudy2026 ::: ::: {#tbl-temperature-beliefs .cell tbl-cap='Beliefs about past and future global temperature change and its causes. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
Please indicate your beliefs about changes in the world’s temperature.
Do you think that the world’s temperature probably has been going up over the past 100 years, or do you think this probably has not been happening? 0 = Extremely certain that the world’s temperature has NOT been going up … 100 = Extremely certain that the world’s temperature has been going up
If nothing is done to prevent it, do you think the world’s temperature probably will go up over the next 100 years, or do you think the world’s temperature probably will not go up over the next 100 years? 0 = Extremely certain that the world’s temperature will NOT go up … 100 = Extremely certain that the world’s temperature will go up
Assuming the world’s temperature did increase over the past 100 years, do you think this increase was caused mostly by things people did, mostly by natural causes, or about equally by things people did and by natural causes? 0 = Mostly by things people did … 100 = Mostly by natural causes
````` ::: ::: ### Risk perception of climate change / Climate change concern #### Option A ::: callout-note From @vlasceanuAddressingClimateChange2024. ::: ::: {#tbl-belief-crisis .cell tbl-cap='Climate change concern. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
How accurate do you think these statements are?
Taking action to fight climate change is necessary to avoid a global catastrophe 0 = Not at all accurate … 100 = Extremely accurate
Climate change poses a serious threat to humanity 0 = Not at all accurate … 100 = Extremely accurate
Climate change is a global emergency 0 = Not at all accurate … 100 = Extremely accurate
````` ::: ::: #### Option B ::: callout-note From @voelkelRegisteredReportMegastudy2026 ::: ::: {#tbl-climate-concern .cell tbl-cap='Climate change concern and perceived importance. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
Please indicate your views on the following questions.
How concerned are you about climate change? 0 = Not at all … 100 = Extremely
How serious a problem is climate change? 0 = Not at all … 100 = Extremely
Relative to other issues facing the U.S., how important is climate change? 0 = The least important issue … 100 = The most important issue
````` ::: ::: ### Trust in specific scientific institutions ::: {#tbl-institutional-trust .cell tbl-cap='Trust in institutions conducting climate science research. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
All of the institutions below conduct research on climate science. How much do you trust these institutions to provide accurate information?
Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Universities and colleges 0 = Not at all … 100 = Very strongly
````` ::: ::: ### Perceived personal benefits of science ::: callout-note Adapted from @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, but we only rely on one item, and use a slider instead of a Likert scale ::: "In your opinion, how much does climate science benefit people like yourself in the United States?" \[Slider, 0 = not at all, 100 = very strongly\] ### Importance of trusting climate scientists ::: callout-note Open-ended question ::: Do you think trusting climate scientists is important for society? Please share your thoughts and arguments on why or why not. \[Text box\] ### Trust in different types of climate scientists ::: callout-note Loosely based on @gligoricHowSocialEvaluations2024. The different types are pretty arbitrary, please suggest changes (also for the wording). ::: Scientists from various fields are conducting research on climate change. How much do you trust scientists studying climate change from each of these fields to provide accurate information? - Atmospheric scientists - Ecologists - Geologists - Meteorologists - Oceanographers - Glaciologists - Environmental scientists - Physicists - Biologists - Chemists - Geoscientists - Environmental engineers - Political scientists - Psychologists - Economists - Sociologists - Energy systems researchers - Social scientists \[Slider, 0 = not at all, 100 = very strongly\] # End of Survey Thank you for participating in this study! Do you have any comments for us? Please write your comments below. \[Text box\] Please click "next" to be redirected to your panel for your payment.
